### My Experience with Harvard CS50: Introduction to Cybersecurity

During my summer break, I found myself with free time and a budding interest in cybersecurity. So, I decided to enroll in the Harvard CS50 Introduction to Cybersecurity course. Here’s a detailed recap of my learning journey — a personal experience, so take it as a subjective account!

* * *

#### Week 0: Foundations & Password Security

The course started strong with a solid introduction to cybersecurity fundamentals. I appreciated learning the rationale behind everyday security practices—like why passwords should be at least 8 characters, why password hints are discouraged, and why arbitrary password changes are a bad idea. Until then, I had been following such rules blindly without questioning them, so understanding the reasons behind them was enlightening.

The week covered common attack types such as dictionary attacks, brute-force attempts, keylogging, and credential stuffing. Multi-factor authentication and tools like password managers and passkeys were introduced as modern defenses.

Overall, the concepts were approachable, and the assignments were manageable for a beginner.

* * *

#### Week 1: Hashing, Salting & Cryptography

Week 1 dove deeper into core concepts, including one-way hash functions and salting—important techniques for securely storing passwords. I must admit, I was initially confused about how hashes are one-way functions, but the explanations clarified this.

The lectures then moved to cryptography basics: symmetric and asymmetric encryption. I was particularly fascinated by asymmetric cryptography and digital signatures—how mathematics can create linked public/private key pairs to ensure security and authenticity. The course tied this nicely into real-world applications like web authentication.

The topic of encryption in transit (TLS), secure deletion, and full-disk encryption was covered as well. I learned that deleting files doesn’t actually erase the data—just the pointers—hence secure deletion techniques overwrite data to prevent recovery. Full-disk encryption adds an extra layer of protection.

Lastly, ransomware was introduced, which was eye-opening—attackers don’t just steal data; they encrypt it and demand ransom to release it. Definitely a sophisticated threat.

The Week 1 assignment was more challenging than Week 0, but still manageable.

* * *

#### Week 2: Network Security & Protocols

This week expanded to network security topics:

- Wi-Fi encryption basics

- The importance of HTTPS over HTTP

- Packet sniffing and man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks

- Cookies and session hijacking

I found the explanations of how HTTPS uses TLS and how digital certificates (issued by trusted browser vendors) work particularly interesting. The course covered SSL stripping attacks and how to mitigate them.

Other topics included VPNs, SSH, network ports and scanning, penetration testing fundamentals, firewalls, and proxies. The idea of a proxy acting as a "friendly" MITM for packet inspection shifted my perspective on network security tools.

The lecture also touched on URL rewriting, malware types (viruses, worms), botnets, DDoS attacks, and antivirus software.

This was the longest lecture (~2 hours), and the assignment was notably more difficult, requiring more effort.

* * *

#### Week 3: Web Security Vulnerabilities

Week 3 focused on web security vulnerabilities:

- Phishing attacks

- Cross-site scripting (XSS): reflected, stored, and mitigation via character escaping

- SQL injection and mitigation via prepared statements

- Command injection

- Cross-site request forgery (CSRF)

I found XSS and CSRF particularly intriguing and researched more online to deepen my understanding (don’t worry, I’m no hacker!).

Additional topics included arbitrary code execution, buffer overflows, and application permissions on app stores and package managers.

The assignment was challenging; I had to Google some answers due to the complexity of the concepts.

* * *

#### Week 4: Privacy, Fingerprinting & Anonymity

The final week covered:

- Server logs and how they are used for diagnostics and analytics

- Privacy considerations and how some software suppresses sensitive info (e.g., hiding search queries)

- Browser fingerprinting, including IP, browser type/version, OS, etc.

- Cookies (session, tracking, third-party, and "supercookies")

- Private browsing modes and what “incognito” really means

- Domain Name System (DNS) basics

- Tor (The Onion Router) for anonymity

- App permissions (like location access in Google Maps)

The course wrapped up nicely here, and the final assignment was manageable.

* * *

#### Final Project

The last requirement was a final project involving researching a cybersecurity topic and presenting it via a slide deck and a YouTube video.

* * *

#### Final Thoughts

Overall, I thoroughly enjoyed this course. It provided a great introduction to cybersecurity fundamentals, blending theory with real-world examples. My interest has grown, especially in offensive security, and I’m planning to continue my journey by taking pentesting courses on Hack The Box.

Hopefully, I’ll be able to share writeups and success stories soon. Until then, thanks for reading!
